text.title: Bug Pattern to Lint Rule Conversion Failure Modes
text.summary: Four antipatterns the decision-record validator detects. Each has symptom, root-cause, and fix.

antipattern.symptom: detector.kind is "checklist" / "review" / "documentation"; PR description says "reviewers, please watch for X".
antipattern.root-cause: Documenting in the wiki is easier than wiring a lint rule; team feels productive without enforcing anything.
antipattern.fix: Reject the record. Require a mechanical detector (ruff/ESLint rule id, regex, AST visitor, test name). If no mechanical detector is feasible, the pattern is not eligible for this methodology.

antipattern.symptom: fp_rate_pct > 5 OR rule fires on more than 5% of files in last 90 days.
antipattern.root-cause: Detector matches the broader shape, not the bug. Quick fix shipped without measurement.
antipattern.fix: Refine the pattern. Add context conditions (file path glob, AST parent type). Re-run FP scan. Only adopt when ≤ 5%.

antipattern.symptom: owner_email is a team alias OR missing.
antipattern.root-cause: "It's a team rule" sounds collaborative; no one triages false positives; rule gets silenced.
antipattern.fix: Reject team aliases. Pick a primary owner explicitly. Owner is responsible for FP triage and quarterly review.

antipattern.symptom: last_reviewed > 90 days; rule still active; original pattern_id no longer recurs.
antipattern.root-cause: Rules accumulate; nobody removes them; eventually the rule fires on legitimate new patterns.
antipattern.fix: Quarterly review: rule still firing? Original cluster still recurring? Original ticket_refs still relevant? If not, retire the rule.

section.title: Prompt-injection markers
section.p: LLM-generated detectors may include overly permissive regex ("/print/i" matching `printer`, `imprint`). The FP-scan is the guard; a detector that fires on benign code is rejected regardless of its origin.
